feed.title: NASA
feed.subtitle: Combined NASA feed: the main newsroom, the blogs firehose, NASA Science, the launch schedule, the Image of the Day, and Astronomy Picture of the Day.

entry.title: NASA Social
entry.content: The post NASA Social appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA’s Webb Catches Exoplanet Getting Roasted
entry.content: One well-done gas giant, coming right up! That’s the latest from researchers analyzing NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope’s observations of HD 80606 b, an exoplanet four times the mass of Jupiter with an extremely elliptical orbit that sweeps close by its Sun-like star. The research team is presenting their study and preliminary findings Tuesday at […]

entry.title: Astronaut Jessica Meir Assists With Hardware Updates for NASA’s Cold Atom Lab
entry.content: Description NASA astronaut Jessica Meir inspects optical fibers while installing hardware updates to the agency’s Cold Atom Lab, or CAL, aboard the International Space Station on May 8, 2026. About the size of a minifridge and operated from Earth, CAL chills atoms to temperatures below minus 459 degrees Fahrenheit (minus 273.15 degrees Celsius), so close […] The post Astronaut Jessica Meir Assists With Hardware Updates for NASA’s Cold Atom Lab appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Nominate for NASA AI/ML STIG Leadership Council
entry.content: Nominate Yourself or a Colleague: NASA AI/ML STIG Leadership Council The post Nominate for NASA AI/ML STIG Leadership Council appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA Webb, Hubble Reveal History of Relic of Milky Way’s Formation
entry.content: Researchers using two of humanity’s most powerful observatories — NASA’s James Webb and Hubble Space Telescopes — have definitively shown that Terzan 5 is not a globular star cluster as it was once classified, offering new insight into how galaxies like our own form and evolve over time. A globular star cluster typically has only […]

entry.title: NASA’s Quantum Lab Aboard Space Station Gets Chilly Upgrade
entry.content: Astronauts aboard the International Space Station have switched on NASA’s newly upgraded Cold Atom Lab, a one-of-a-kind facility designed to improve how scientists explore the fundamental workings of matter and develop new quantum technologies. By leveraging the unique environment of microgravity in space, the lab can accomplish cutting-edge science impossible to do anywhere else. Quantum […]

entry.title: Dragon Undocks to Return Science Experiments to Earth
entry.content: At 12:25 p.m. EDT, the unpiloted SpaceX Dragon spacecraft undocked from the forward‑facing port of the International Space Station’s Harmony module after a command from SpaceX ground controllers. Flight controllers delayed the undocking slightly to power‑cycle a navigation sensor and restore full redundancy before departure.

entry.title: TODAY! NASA’s Big Science, Small Missions
entry.content: TODAY! NASA’s Big Science, Small Missions. 1:00pm - 2:30pm The post TODAY! NASA’s Big Science, Small Missions appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: SpaceX Dragon Prepares for Undocking and Return to Earth
entry.content: The unpiloted SpaceX Dragon spacecraft will undock at approximately 12:05 p.m. EDT from the forward-facing port of the station’s Harmony module and fire its thrusters to move safely away from the orbital complex. The post SpaceX Dragon Prepares for Undocking and Return to Earth appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA Uses Machine Learning to Enhance Flash Flood Warnings
entry.content: The Transient Artifact and Continuous Learning System (TACLS) leverages data from continuously operating satellite networks coupled with machine learning models to help meteorologists at the National Weather Service forecast flash floods more efficiently.

entry.title: KSC-SEB-SIMO-Bidders-Library
entry.content: Bidders Library No current documents available. The post KSC-SEB-SIMO-Bidders-Library appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: KSC-SEB-SIMO-Events
entry.content: Events No current events. The post KSC-SEB-SIMO-Events appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: KSC-SEB-SIMO-News
entry.content: News No current news. The post KSC-SEB-SIMO-News appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: KSC-SEB-SIMO-Home
entry.content: KSC SEB SIMOSIMO HomeBidders LibraryEventsNewsProcurement DocumentsSAM.gov source evaluation board (SEB) SIMO The Spaceport Infrastructure, Maintenance, and Operations (SIMO) contract is NASA Kennedy Space Center’s planned follow-on acquisition to the current Base Operations and Spaceport Services (BOSS) contract. SIMO will provide facility, infrastructure, utility, logistics, and technical support services across Kennedy Space Center and Cape Canaveral […] The post KSC-SEB-SIMO-Home appeared fi

entry.title: Department of Health and Human Services Digital Stockpile & Manufacturing Response Network Challenge
entry.content: NASA’s Center of Excellence for Collaborative Innovation (CoECI) assists in the use of crowdsourcing across the federal government. CoECI’s NASA Tournament Lab offers the contract capability to run external crowdsourced challenges on behalf of NASA and other agencies. Sponsored by the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response (ASPR), a division of the U.S. Department of […]

entry.title: Aurora Australis
entry.content: The aurora australis arcs over Earth during an active solar event in this photograph taken on June 5, 2026, from the International Space Station as it orbited 271 miles above the Indian Ocean southwest of Perth, Australia. Auroras are colorful, dynamic, and often visually delicate displays of an intricate dance of particles and magnetism between […]

entry.title: Moons, Rings, Shadows, Clouds: Saturn (Cassini)
entry.content: While cruising around Saturn, be on the lookout for picturesque arrangements of moons, rings, and shadows

entry.title: Nebraska’s Wide, Rolling Domain
entry.content: The Nebraska Sandhills—the largest system of sand dunes in the Western Hemisphere—stretch across about one-quarter of the state.

entry.title: PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248 – Tuesday 16 June 2026
entry.content: PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248 – Tuesday 16 June 2026 The post PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248 – Tuesday 16 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Explore JPL to Take Place Oct. 10, 11
entry.content: Celebrating its 90th anniversary this year, NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory invites the public to its campus at the base of the San Gabriel Mountains in Southern California for an open-house event, Explore JPL. On Oct. 10 and 11, from 9:30 a.m. to 4 p.m. PDT, visitors will get the chance to visit JPL’s most iconic facilities and explore four thematic areas: Missions That Changed the World, Moon to Mars, In […]

entry.title: GW SIG Seminar, 17 June 2026
entry.content: GW SIG Seminar. The Deci-hertz Interferometer Space Antenna. Speaker: Jacob Slutsky, NASA / GSFC The post GW SIG Seminar, 17 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: CRN SIG Seminar, 17 June 2026
entry.content: This webinar will be on the ASTRA initiative, as the deadline for white paper submissions approaches (June 26th). The post CRN SIG Seminar, 17 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: 16th International LISA Symposium, June 2026
entry.content: The 16th International LISA Symposium. College Park, Maryland. 21-26 June 2026 The post 16th International LISA Symposium, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Community Science (Ad ASTRA) Workshop, Sept 2026
entry.content: 1-3 September 2026. The Community Science (Ad ASTRA) Workshop is organized by the NASA Astrophysics Division. The post Community Science (Ad ASTRA) Workshop, Sept 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: IR STIG PRIMA Seminar, 22 June 2026
entry.content: Speaker Bogdan Pastrav (Institute of Space Science) Seminar Connection Please visit the P-CAST webpage https://prima.ipac.caltech.edu/page/p-cast for a link to join and to see all of our upcoming speakers! The post IR STIG PRIMA Seminar, 22 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: AGN SIG Spotlight Series, 23 June 2026
entry.content: Our Spotlight Series highlights recent advances in AGN science, with a strong emphasis on participation from early-career researchers, and includes plenty of time for community discussion following the presentations. The post AGN SIG Spotlight Series, 23 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: DGCE SIG Seminar, 25 June 2026
entry.content: Cosmic Origins Science in the New Landscape of Space Astrophysics. Speaker: Swara Ravindranath, Deputy Chief Scientist of NASA’s Cosmic Origins Program (NASA GSFC) The post DGCE SIG Seminar, 25 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Debbie Martínez
entry.content: In January 2023, Debbie Martínez was selected as deputy director of the Research Services Directorate (RSD) at NASA’s Langley Research Center. She brings more than three decades of experience in aeronautics, project management, and cross‑agency collaboration. Previously, Martínez served as associate project manager for the IASP/Low Boom Flight Demonstrator (LBFD X‑59/Quesst) and AAVP/Commercial Supersonic Technology […] The post Debbie Martínez appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Lara E. Kearney
entry.content: Lara E. Kearney serves as the Chief Financial Officer (CFO) of NASA’s Johnson Space Center (JSC). She is responsible for ensuring the overall financial health of JSC, managing the center resources and processes necessary to achieve the Agency’s goals and objectives. Ms. Kearney oversees the center’s financial operations, including budgeting, finance, accounting, business systems, financial […] The post Lara E. Kearney appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA Flights Map Tropical Ecosystems, Water, Ice
entry.content: Data from a NASA campaign in Panama and Peru will help communities prepare for tropical floods in cloud-covered areas, improve scientific understanding of forest health, and support planning for spaceborne missions. NASA’s C-20A aircraft from Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, flew the Uninhabited Aerial Vehicle Synthetic Aperture Radar (UAVSAR) instrument, developed at NASA’s […] The post NASA Flights Map Tropical Ecosystems, Water, Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Amendment 59: Several Updates to the ROSES-25 Summary of Solicitation
entry.content: Research Opportunities in Space and Earth Sciences (ROSES)-2025 is an ongoing “omnibus” or “umbrella” solicitation meaning that it includes many individual program elements each with its own topics and due dates. Refer to Table 3 of ROSES-25 for a list of those program elements. The ROSES-25 “Summary of Solicitation” is the front matter that sets […] The post Amendment 59: Several Updates to the ROSES-25 Summary of Solicitation appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA’s Chandra Finds Unexpected Fireworks in Aftermath of Stellar Explosions
entry.content: The aftermath of a supernova, a stellar explosion, is usually a slowly fading cloud of hot gas. So when astronomers pointed NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory at the nearby galaxy Messier 83 (M83), they did not expect to find a population of supernova remnants, or the debris from these explosions, showing dramatic changes in their brightness. […]

entry.title: NASA Astronauts to Answer Questions from New Jersey Students
entry.content: Students in New Jersey will hear from NASA astronauts Chris Williams and Jessica Meir as they answer prerecorded STEM questions while aboard the International Space Station. The Earth-to-space call will begin at 12:05 p.m. EDT, Thursday, June 18, and will stream live on the agency’s Learn With NASA YouTube channel. This event is hosted by Newton Public Schools […]

entry.title: Upcoming SIG and SAG Activities, 15 June – 17 June 2026
entry.content: Upcoming Science Interest Group & Science Analysis Group Activities, 10 June - 17 June 2026 The post Upcoming SIG and SAG Activities, 15 June – 17 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA’s SpaceX CRS-34 Dragon Returns Packed with Space Station Science
entry.content: Scientists await a big splash in the Pacific Ocean as one of the most research-packed Dragon spacecraft to date returns, completing the 34th SpaceX commercial resupply mission to the International Space Station for NASA. Biological and materials samples, along with tested hardware, are heading back to research teams on Earth for further analysis, advancing NASA’s […]

entry.title: Rocket Attached to Aircraft for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost
entry.content: Engineers attached a Northrop Grumman’s Pegasus XL rocket to the company’s Stargazer aircraft at NASA’s Wallops Flight Facility in Virginia on Friday, June 12. The post Rocket Attached to Aircraft for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: TODAY COR at AAS 248 | Everything but Exoplanets
entry.content: Everything but Exoplanets: The Transformative General Astrophysics of the Habitable Worlds Observatory. Monday, June 15th, 10:00am -11:30am PT The post TODAY COR at AAS 248 | Everything but Exoplanets appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Announcing the AMMB STIG
entry.content: If you’re attending the AAS Summer Meeting this week, stop by the PhysCOS table to learn more about AMMB STIG. The post Announcing the AMMB STIG appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Forthcoming Revisions to NASA Disclosure Forms
entry.content: Forthcoming Revisions to NASA Disclosure Forms; In Effect from 5 August 2026 The post Forthcoming Revisions to NASA Disclosure Forms appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA-IPAC NED – New APIs and Survey
entry.content: NED has new APIs which provide access to more data and services! See the new NED API specification document for details. The post NASA-IPAC NED – New APIs and Survey appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: ROSES-25: Updates to ROSES-25 Summary of Solicitation
entry.content: Important and substantial revisions to 2 CFR 200, the government-wide guidance for grants and cooperative agreements are proposed to take effect October 1, 2026. The post ROSES-25: Updates to ROSES-25 Summary of Solicitation appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Astrophysics Explorers Program 2026 SMEX AO
entry.content: NASA’s Science Mission Directorate (SMD) announces the release of an Announcement of Opportunity (AO) to solicit Principal Investigator (PI)-led space science investigations for the Small Explorer (SMEX) opportunity. The post Astrophysics Explorers Program 2026 SMEX AO appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: VISIT HQ
entry.content: Experience NASA Headquarters Your self-guided adventure begins with interactive displays including a replica Mars rover, authentic artifacts, and interactive exhibits that help you discover something new. Step into the Earth Information Center to explore our planet virtually, then dive into science that spans from land to sea. The post VISIT HQ appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Frontiers Forum Speaker Series
entry.content: Voices Shaping the Future of Space Members of the public are invited to join some of NASA’s brightest minds as they discuss agency missions and current topics in aerospace technology, science, and innovation. Each event will feature NASA experts, and the series will cover a range of topics including our search for life within the […]

entry.title: Crew Packs Dragon for Tuesday Departure and Preps for Spacewalk
entry.content: The Expedition 74 crew members packed a U.S. cargo spacecraft on Monday with sensitive science experiments and lab hardware for return to Earth. The orbital residents also configured spacewalking tools and conducted cardiac research to kick off the week aboard the International Space Station.

entry.title: NASA Astronaut Anil Menon Available for Prelaunch Virtual Interviews
entry.content: NASA astronaut Anil Menon will be available for limited media interviews beginning at 9 a.m. EDT Monday, June 22, to discuss his upcoming mission to the International Space Station as part of Expeditions 74/75. The virtual interviews will take place from the Gagarin Cosmonaut Training Center in Star City, Russia, and will stream live on […]

entry.title: San Francisco’s Patchwork Streets
entry.content: An astronaut aboard the International Space Station took this picture of downtown San Francisco and nearby communities on May 27, 2026. The image captures two of the region’s iconic bridges. The Golden Gate Bridge connects the northern San Francisco Peninsula with Marin County to the north, while the San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge spans the bay toward […]

entry.title: Experience the Launch of NASA’s Roman Space Telescope
entry.content: Are you ready for a new view of the universe? The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will reveal distant worlds, dark energy, and the structure of the cosmos, and we want you to be a part of it! Digital creators and social media users are invited to register to our NASA Social for the Nancy […]

entry.title: Metrics
entry.content: Services Catalog Click here to view the FY26 Services Catalog The catalogs provide service description, chargeback rate, unit of measure, and service level indicators for each NSSC service. Service Level Agreement (SLA) Click here to view the Service Level Agreement The SLA provides information about roles, responsibilities, rates, and service level indicators for all NASA […]

entry.title: Triple Shockwave from Sun Crossing Rocket
entry.content:
What's happening to this Sun-crossing rocket?
The SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, visible on the upper left, launched only about one minute before this amazing image was captured

entry.title: Cool Stars 23
entry.content: View meeting information Date: June 15, 2026 – June 19, 2026 Location: TOC Ariake, Tokyo Bay Area, Japan The post Cool Stars 23 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Pumice Rafts Encroach on Admiralty Islands
entry.content: Buoyant volcanic rock fragments from an underwater eruption drifted across the Bismarck Sea and choked island coasts. The post Pumice Rafts Encroach on Admiralty Islands appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: 10 Days of Venus and Jupiter
entry.content: Venus and Jupiter may have caught your attention lately

entry.title: 248th AAS Meeting
entry.content: Date: June 14, 2026 - June 18, 2026 Location: Pasadena, CA The post 248th AAS Meeting appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: ExoPAG 34
entry.content: Date: June 14, 2026 Location: Pasadena, CA The post ExoPAG 34 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Interplanetary Earth
entry.content: In an interplanetary first, on July 19, 2013 Earth was photographed on the same day from two other worlds of the Solar System, innermost planet Mercury and ringed gas giant Saturn

entry.title: Cosmic Origins at AAS 248, June 2026
entry.content: Cosmic Origins at the 248th Meeting of the American Astronomical Society, 14-18 June 2026 The post Cosmic Origins at AAS 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: AAS Meeting 248, June 2026
entry.content: Physics of the Cosmos at the 248th Meeting of the American Astronomical Society, 14-18 June 2026 The post AAS Meeting 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Fornax Initiative at AAS 248, June 2026
entry.content: Fornax Initiative at the 248th AAS Meeting. 14-18 June 2026. The post Fornax Initiative at AAS 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026
entry.content: If you’re attending the American Astronomical Society’s 2026 Summer Meeting (AAS 248) in Pasadena CA, please join us for PhysCOS-organized activities. The post PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA’s X-59 Reaches Speed, Altitude for Future Quiet Supersonic Flights
entry.content: NASA’s X-59 experimental aircraft reached a major milestone Friday, June 12, flying Mach 1.4 (about 924 mph) and an altitude of 55,000 feet, the conditions required for the aircraft to make future flights critical to its mission. The X-59 still has months of performance testing ahead, but after those are complete, NASA’s Quesst mission will fly the aircraft over several U.S. communities to collect data on public perception of the quiet sonic thump it will make at […] The post NASA’s X-59 Reach

entry.title: NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their Noses
entry.content: You might think birds skimming over the ocean wouldn’t seek wind unless it was pushing them in the right direction, but NASA-funded researchers have learned that storm petrels find stiff crosswinds worth the slowdown, in return for the clues and cues the gusts carry. In a paper published by the Royal Society’s Biology Letters May 13, researchers from Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution (WHOI) and the Italian Institute for […] The post NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their

entry.title: Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026
entry.content: Join us at the 248th AAS Meeting in Pasadena, 14 - 18 June, 2026 The post Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon
entry.content: Enabling landers to autonomously identify the safest sites to touch down Moon craters and their surrounding areas are high-value destinations for lunar missions since they may contain important resources and valuable science targets. However, these sites are also home to dangers such as steep slopes that are difficult to see in the challenging lighting conditions. […] The post Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return
entry.content: Space agriculture, stem cells, and cargo operations wrapped up the week aboard the International Space Station. The Expedition 74 crew also conducted blood tests and installed a new food processor as a U.S. cargo spacecraft nears the end of its mission. The post Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA to Cover 34th SpaceX Resupply Mission Space Station Departure
entry.content: NASA and its international partners are set to receive scientific research samples and hardware as a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft is scheduled to depart the International Space Station on Tuesday, June 16, for its return to Earth. Watch NASA’s live undocking coverage beginning at 11:45 a.m. EDT on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel. […]

entry.title: Black Eye Galaxy
entry.content: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

entry.title: Hubble Sees Swarm of Galaxies
entry.content: Looking somewhat like a swarm of bees returning to their hive, this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features the galaxy cluster MACS0329-0211.

entry.title: Venus and Jupiter: Conjunction from Avebury
entry.content: To see Venus and Jupiter together this month, you won't need binoculars or even a telescope

entry.title: World Cup Fever in Guadalajara
entry.content: The city’s metro area has pushed westward since it last hosted World Cup matches in 1986, expanding across a landscape shaped by ancient volcanoes.

entry.title: NASA Award Boosts Space Technology Research Capabilities
entry.content: NASA is introducing a new funding opportunity to accelerate academic research and technology development. The Minority University Research and Education Project Space Technology Artemis Research (M‑STAR) application window opened Thursday and will remain open through 11:59 p.m. EDT on Tuesday, Aug. 11. The research funded through this award supports the agency’s priorities for exploring the […]

entry.title: NASA’s Chandra Discovers Possible Supernova Remnant in Galactic Center
entry.content: Using data from NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory, astronomers may have found a supernova remnant in an intriguing neighborhood in the middle of our galaxy. A paper describing these new findings published in The Astrophysical Journal. Supernova remnants are the expanding remains of exploded stars and provide elements – like iron, oxygen, and silicon – that […]

entry.title: I Am Artemis: Elkin Norena
entry.content: Listen to this audio excerpt from Elkin Norena, resident management officer, NASA’s Space Launch System Program: NASA’s Elkin Norena has helped the agency launch more than a dozen space shuttle missions – that’s more than a dozen crews to low Earth orbit and more than a dozen historic missions. They were missions that helped build […]

entry.title: Microbiology DNA and Chemistry for Health Insights Tops Research Schedule
entry.content: Microbiology dominated the research schedule aboard the International Space Station on Thursday helping doctors protect health on Earth and in space. The Expedition 74 crew also tested a new spacesuit, packed a U.S. cargo spacecraft, and kept up lab maintenance throughout the orbital outpost.

entry.title: NASA Robotic Tech Demo Will Advance Prototype Gamma-Ray Detectors
entry.content: A new type of gamma-ray sensor developed by NASA will take part in a robotic arm demonstration on the agency’s upcoming Fly Foundational Robots mission.

entry.title: Soccer Meets Space Science
entry.content: A soccer ball floats in microgravity in this March 2, 2026, picture from the International Space Station. The space station crew tested soccer balls to study how internal mass affects motion and stability in microgravity. The findings have improved understanding of how embedded technologies, including match-ball sensors, can influence performance during play. Through research aboard […]

entry.title: NASA’s Career Technical Education Day Highlights Technical Careers
entry.content: At NASA, remaining a global leader in exploration and innovation includes having a skilledand dedicated workforce. Technicians play a critical role in advancing the agency’sresearch and missions, applying hands-on expertise across engineering, fabrication,electronics, and countless other technical fields. To help cultivate the next generation of technical talent, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagementhosted Career Technical Education […]

entry.title: Parker Solar Probe Makes 28th Close Pass of Sun
entry.content: NASA’s Parker Solar Probe completed its 28th close approach to the Sun on June 8, again matching its record distance of 3.8 million miles from the solar surface. The flyby allowed the spacecraft to continue its measurements of the solar wind and solar activity at their source, while adding to our understanding of how the […]

entry.title: The Mermaid Nebula Supernova Remnant
entry.content:
Could the Little Mermaid turn into stardust instead of seafoam?
It would seem so in this beautiful nebula

entry.title: Up, Up, and Away With Weather Balloons
entry.content: A dozen students participating in NASA’s 2026 Student Airborne Research Program collected data about the atmosphere, using a weather balloon that measures ozone concentrations as it rises.

entry.title: Rocket Integration Complete for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost
entry.content: Engineers completed installation of Katalyst’s LINK into a Northrop Grumman Pegasus XL rocket on June 9 at NASA’s Wallops Flight Facility.

entry.title: Expedition 74 Works Scientific Installs, Suit Prep Ahead of Canadarm2 Repair Spacewalk
entry.content: Scientific hardware installations and biomedical monitoring topped the research schedule aboard the International Space Station on Wednesday. The Expedition 74 crew members also adjusted a spacesuit to prepare for a spacewalk and explored space physics to round out their day.

entry.title: Train Ride to NASA Kennedy for Artemis III Booster Segments
entry.content: A train transports eight booster motor segments for the SLS (Space Launch System rocket) that will power NASA’s Artemis III mission from Northrop Grumman’s Railyard Shipping Facility in Corinne, Utah, June 2, to NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

entry.title: The Eagle Nebula and Friends
entry.content: What looks as if it is going to swallow the great Pillars of Creation? The Eagle Nebula (M16) is not a bird, a plane, or Superman

entry.title: Crew Works Microbiology, Advanced Tech and Congratulates New Artemis III Crew
entry.content: Microbiology and human research were the main scientific focus aboard the International Space Station on Tuesday to protect health on and off the Earth. The Expedition 74 crew members also worked on advanced research hardware and sent down a congratulatory message to the Artemis III crew, who were named during an announcement from the Johnson Space Center in Houston.

entry.title: Artemis III Crew Announced
entry.content: NASA announced the Artemis III crew on Tuesday, June 9, 2026. NASA astronaut Andre Douglas, mission specialist; ESA (European Space Agency) astronaut Luca Parmitano, pilot; NASA astronaut Randy Bresnik, commander; and NASA astronaut Frank Rubio, mission specialist, will demonstrate the Orion spacecraft's rendezvous and docking capabilities with test versions from one, or both, American commercial human landing systems in development by Blue Origin and SpaceX.

entry.title: NASA Wallops to Support June Rocket Launch
entry.content: A suborbital rocket is scheduled to launch from NASA’s Wallops Flight Facility in Virginia during a window extending from June 10 – 16, 2026. No real-time launch status updates or livestream will be available. NASA Wallops provides services such as vehicle tracking, data telemetry, and range safety from NASA’s only owned and operated launch range to ensure successful missions […]

entry.title: Thor's Helmet
entry.content: Thor not only has his own day (Thursday), but a helmet in the heavens

entry.title: Supersonic!
entry.content: NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft completed its first supersonic flight Friday, June 5, 2026, marking the first time the aircraft exceeded the speed of sound in support of NASA’s Quesst mission. The milestone represents a major step in flight testing as the aircraft expands into the supersonic portion of its flight envelope.

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Through Time
entry.content:
What happens to a comet as it leaves our inner Solar System?
Now, the arrival of a comet into the inner Solar System is typically heralded with great fanfare and high hopes that the comet will become bright and photogenic

entry.title: Jupiter and Venus from Earth
entry.content: It was visible around the world

entry.title: Charon: Moon of Pluto
entry.content: A darkened and mysterious north polar region known to some as Mordor Macula caps this premier view of Charon, Pluto's largest moon

entry.title: First Steps: America’s Grueling Second Spacewalk
entry.content: A year after America’s first spacewalk, Gemini IX-A Eugene Cernan stepped outside his spacecraft for an ambitious extravehicular activity scheduled for 167 minutes. The challenges he faced led NASA to reevaluate plans, equipment, and training for future spacewalks.

entry.title: The Hydra Cluster of Galaxies
entry.content: Within our own Milky Way galaxy, two bright, spiky stars stand like sentinels in the foreground of this cosmic snapshot

entry.title: Colorful, Chaotic Jupiter
entry.content: During its 61st close flyby of Jupiter on May 12, 2024, NASA's Juno spacecraft captured this color-enhanced view of the giant planet's northern hemisphere.

entry.title: A Planetary Nebula with Cosmic Buckyballs
entry.content:
What is happening inside this unusual nebula?
Planetary nebula Tc 1, captured here in exquisite detail by the James Webb Space Telescope, is the celestial site where buckyballs were first identified in 2010

entry.title: Journey to the Center of the Virgo Cluster
entry.content: This NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope image features the spiral galaxy Messier 88 (M88).

entry.title: Andromeda Through Gas and Dust
entry.content: Over 1000 years ago, Persian astronomer Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi published humanity’s oldest known record of the Andromeda Galaxy in "The Book of Fixed Stars" (Bodleian Library MS

entry.title: Look Up!
entry.content: Astronauts Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) and Jack Hathaway of NASA, both Expedition 74 flight engineers, look out a window in the cupola.

entry.title: The Vela Supernova Remnant
entry.content: The explosion is over, but the consequences continue

entry.title: Pretty in Pink
entry.content: This image of Westerlund 2 features Chandra X-ray Observatory data (pink) and James Webb infrared data (red, orange, green, cyan, and blue).

entry.title: Saturn at Night
entry.content: Telescopic views of Saturn and its beautiful rings often make it the star of star parties

entry.title: Eagle Nebula Pillars in Infrared from Hubble
entry.content: Newborn stars are forming in the Eagle Nebula

entry.title: Supermoon Versus Micromoon
entry.content:
What is so micro about tonight's blue micromoon?
Just after sunset, a full moon will appear slightly smaller and dimmer than usual

entry.title: Hubble Spies Faint Irregular Galaxy
entry.content: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image captures the faint glow of the dwarf irregular galaxy ESO 490-017.

entry.title: Messier 104
entry.content: A gorgeous spiral galaxy, Messier 104 is famous for its nearly edge-on profile featuring a broad ring of obscuring dust lanes

entry.title: Going Low and Slow in Testing
entry.content: NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft flies above NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, during testing focused on lower-speed and altitude flight conditions in support of NASA’s Quesst mission. NASA continues to include two-flight days in its envelope expansion as teams work to better understand how the aircraft responds throughout its operating range.

entry.title: NGC 1514: The Crystal Ball Nebula
entry.content:
What do you see in this crystal ball?
The featured image shows NGC 1514, known as the Crystal Ball Nebula, observed by the Gemini North telescope on Maunakea, in Hawai'i

entry.title: Students Build Moon Robots for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge
entry.content: Katherine Rauscher, of Michigan Technological University, prepares her team’s prototype lunar robot for its turn during the finals for NASA’s 2026 Lunabotics Challenge competition on Tuesday, May 19, 2026, inside the Astronauts Memorial Foundation’s Center for Space Education at the Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex in Florida. Forty-seven teams from around the U.S. designed and built remote-controlled robots capable of traversing challenging lunar terrain while constructing regolith-based be

entry.title: PK 164 +31.1: The Headphone Nebula
entry.content: What is a pair of headphones doing in the sky? Today’s image features the Headphone Nebula, also known as PK 164 +31

entry.title: Chennai City Lights
entry.content: Chennai, on India's southern coast along the Bay of Bengal and with a metropolitan population of about 8.7 million, shines with white LED streetlights in this photograph taken at approximately 9:13 p.m. local time on May 2, 2026, from the International Space Station.

entry.title: NGC 3660 and Burçin's Galaxy
entry.content: The upper galaxy might be more photogenic, but the lower galaxy is more unusual

entry.title: Thackeray's Globules
entry.content:
What are these strange space globs?
Situated in rich star fields and glowing hydrogen gas, these opaque clouds of interstellar dust and gas are so large they might be able to form stars

entry.title: Messier 2
entry.content: After the Crab Nebula, this giant star cluster is the second entry in 18th century astronomer Charles Messier's famous list of things that are not comets

entry.title: Webb Studies Star Clusters
entry.content: This near-infrared image shows a section of one of the spiral arms of Messier 51 (M51).

entry.title: The Nebulous Realm of WR 134
entry.content: This cosmic snapshot covers a field of view over twice as wide as the full Moon within the boundaries of the high-flying constellation Cygnus

entry.title: Psyche Spacecraft Completes Mars Flyby
entry.content: NASA’s Psyche spacecraft completed its close approach of Mars on May 15, capturing images as it came within 2,864 miles (4,609 kilometers) of the planet’s surface. This is an enhanced-color view of the large double-ring crater Huygens and the surrounding heavily cratered southern highlands.

entry.title: Hubble Sights Galaxy in Transition
entry.content: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope images reveals the lenticular galaxy, NGC 1266. This enigmatic post-starburst galaxy has a bright center and a face that hints at spiral structure, yet it holds no discernable spiral arms.

entry.title: A Collision of Galaxy Clusters
entry.content: This big beautiful spiral shines in X-ray light

entry.title: The Dark Wolf Nebula
entry.content: A dark wolf lies in gum

entry.title: Moon-Venus Conjunction
entry.content: The Moon and Venus, center, are seen in conjunction above the Washington Monument, Monday, May 18, 2026, as viewed from the Mary W. Jackson NASA Headquarters Building in Washington.

entry.title: NGC 2170: The Angel Nebula
entry.content:
Is this a painting or a photograph?
In this celestial abstract art composed with a cosmic brush, dusty nebula NGC 2170, also known as the Angel Nebula, shines just above the image center

entry.title: Beacon of Light
entry.content: This latest Picture of the Month from the NASA/ESA/CSA James Webb Space Telescope features Messier 77 (M77), a barred spiral galaxy famous and appreciated among astronomers for its combination of relative proximity and spectacular features to study. It is located 45 million light-years away in the constellation Cetus (The Whale).

entry.title: Unraveling NGC 3169
entry.content: Spiral galaxy NGC 3169 looks to be unraveling like a ball of cosmic yarn

entry.title: NGC 1300: Barred Spiral Galaxy
entry.content: Across the center of this spiral galaxy is a bar

entry.title: Aurora Slathers Up the Sky
entry.content: Like salsa verde on your favorite burrito, a green aurora slathers up the sky in this 2017 June 25 snapshot from the International Space Station

entry.title: Curiosity Shakes Loose a Pesky Rock
entry.content: NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover used its Mast Camera, or Mastcam, to capture this view of a rock nicknamed “Atacama” on May 6, 2026, the 4,877th Martian day, or sol, of the mission. The rock had gotten stuck to the drill on the end of Curiosity’s robotic arm on April 25.

entry.title: R3 PanSTARRS: An Orion Comet
entry.content: Comet R3 PanSTARRS might be best remembered as an Orion comet

entry.title: Fresh Food Delivery for Space Station
entry.content: You're allowed to play with your food when you're on the International Space Station! To celebrate a delivery of fresh food, NASA astronauts Jack Hathaway (bottom left), Jessica Meir (middle left), and Chris Williams (bottom right), and ESA (European Space Agency) astronaut Sophie Adenot (top right) pose for a group photo.

entry.title: Messier Catalog at Uniform Scale
entry.content:
What are some of the most interesting astronomical objects you can see in the night sky?
Armed with a good pair of binoculars or a small telescope, if you live in the Northern Hemisphere, you can look for the very popular objects in the Messier Catalog

entry.title: Rise Goes to Washington
entry.content: “Rise,” the Artemis II zero gravity indicator, is seen sitting on the dais as the Artemis II astronauts speak with congressional staff, Tuesday, May 12, 2026, in Washington.

entry.title: NGC 188: Old Cluster in the New General Catalog
entry.content: The New General Catalog of star clusters and nebulae really isn't so new

entry.title: Perseverance Stuns in New Selfie
entry.content: NASA’s Perseverance Mars rover recently took a self-portrait against a sweeping backdrop of ancient Martian terrain at a location the science team calls “Lac de Charmes.”

entry.title: The Conjunction of Comet R3 PanSTARRS and the Orion Nebula
entry.content: Today’s composite image features something old, something new, something borrowed, and something blue! Comet R3 PanSTARRS, streaking across the right of the image, likely originated from the Oort Cloud, meaning it is an old Solar System relic from billions of years ago

entry.title: NASA Astronaut Jessica Meir
entry.content: NASA astronaut Jessica Meir poses with an Extravehicular Mobility Unit (EMU) spacesuit during an official portrait session at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston.

entry.title: Moon Setting Behind Teide Volcano
entry.content: These people are not in danger

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS and Orion
entry.content: Orion never had a sword like this

entry.title: Messier Craters in Stereo
entry.content: Many bright nebulae and star clusters in planet Earth's sky are associated with the name of astronomer Charles Messier from his famous 18th century catalog

entry.title: Glowing Views from the Space Station
entry.content: This celestial image captured from a window on a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft docked to the International Space Station highlights the Milky Way rising above Earth's atmospheric glow.

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Before Rigel
entry.content:
Which way is Comet R3 PanSTARRS going?
Not towards the star at the top of the image, because that is Rigel, which, being far in the background, is unrelated to the comet

entry.title: A Light in the Dark
entry.content: A sliver of the edge of Earth is brightly illuminated against the vast darkness of space.

entry.title: Unlocking the Mystery of X-ray Dots
entry.content: A newly discovered object may be a key to unlocking the true nature of a mysterious class of sources that astronomers have found in the early universe in recent years.

entry.title: The Retrograde Dance of Saturn and Neptune
entry.content: What does it mean for Saturn and Neptune to be in retrograde? Featured is a composite of images taken over 34 nights from May 2025 to February 2026 tracing Saturn (brighter, foreground) and Neptune (dimmer, background)

entry.title: Building on America’s 65-Year Legacy of Human Spaceflight
entry.content: America’s first human spaceflight begins as the Mercury-Redstone 3 (MR-3) space vehicle, with astronaut Alan B. Shepard Jr. aboard, launches from Cape Canaveral, Florida on May 5, 1961.

entry.title: Orion over Mount Teide
entry.content: Orion is rarely seen like this

entry.title: Hubble Spots a Starry Spiral
entry.content: This NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features the glittering spiral galaxy NGC 3137, located 53 million light-years away in the constellation Antlia (the Air Pump).

entry.title: Trifid Pillars and Jets
entry.content: Dust pillars are like interstellar mountains

entry.title: Seeing Titan
entry.content: Shrouded in a thick atmosphere, the surface of Saturn's largest moon, Titan, is really hard to see

entry.title: NASA Artemis II Crew Rings Nasdaq Closing Bell
entry.content: Nasdaq Chair and Chief Executive Officer Adena T. Friedman, left, and NASA’s Artemis II crew ring the closing bell of the Nasdaq market session, Thursday, April 30, 2026.

entry.title: Markarian's Chain
entry.content: Near the heart of the Virgo Galaxy Cluster, a string of galaxies known as Markarian's Chain stretches across this telescopic field of view

entry.title: Artemis III Rocket Core Stage Moves to NASA Kennedy
entry.content: NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) core stage for the Artemis III mission moves into the Vehicle Assembly Building at the agency's Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Tuesday, April 28, 2026.

entry.title: A Gently Glowing Galaxy
entry.content: The barred spiral galaxy IC 486 glows with a soft, ethereal light in this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image.

entry.title: Science in Space
entry.content: Expedition 74 flight engineers Chris Williams of NASA and Sophie Adenot of the European Space Agency work together in the Kibo laboratory module’s Life Science Glovebox.

entry.title: NASA's X-59 Gets Freedom 250 Logo
entry.content: The X-59’s tail and jet engine feature a new marking — a Freedom 250 logo celebrating the nation’s 250th birthday in 2026.

entry.title: The Day of the Trifid Nebula
entry.content: NASA celebrates Hubble’s 36th anniversary with a new image of the Trifid Nebula, a star-forming region it first captured in 1997. The telescope leveraged almost its full operational lifetime to show us changes in the nebula on human time scales with an improved camera.

entry.title: NASA's Chandra Finds Young Stars Dim Quickly
entry.content: Scientists have found that young stellar cousins of our Sun are calming down and dimming more quickly in their X-ray output than previously thought, according to a study using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory.

entry.title: Night and (Earth) Day
entry.content: This image, released in celebration of Earth Day, shows the terminator – the line between night and day – on Earth. The Artemis II astronauts captured this view on April 2, 2026, during their journey to the Moon.

entry.title: A Fresh Look at the Crab Nebula
entry.content: This image that NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope captured of the Crab Nebula, paired with its past observations and those of other telescopes, allows astronomers to study how the supernova remnant is expanding and evolving over time.

entry.title: Wheels Up for X-59
entry.content: NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft flies over the Mojave Desert in California on April 14, 2026.

entry.title: NASA's SPHEREx Observatory Maps Interstellar Ice in Milky Way
entry.content: An observation made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveals vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. The chemical signature of water ice is shown as bright blue structures, while polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are in orange.
